rationale: This is the classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, showing the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (H2SO4) conditions. This carbocation is then trapped by the simplest liquid nitrile, acetonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to yield N-tert-butylacetamide. All components are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: The use of a simple tertiary alcohol (tert-butanol) and a simple nitrile (acetonitrile) avoids complex structures that could overlap with other named reactions. It specifically avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (Markovnikov addition variants), or other functional groups that might trigger different reaction family classifications. The reactants and product are small and focused on the key C-N bond formation characteristic of the Ritter reaction.
